author: *Solaris* 4NUNN4KI
content:
Hi.
Will it be possible to manage the habs via multiple wallets, or only through the owner's wallet?
This question concerns those who purchased habitats as a group.
When I speak of "managing habs via multiple wallets," I am referring to crafting, crews, access rights, and any other habs use cases.
I am not asking about every design aspect right now, but simply whether or not group management of habs across multiple wallets will be possible.
Thanks.

It is the in-game access aspect that interests me here - specifically, for a hab, having access to it via various addresses that have been pre-authorized to interact with it. Am I on the right track, or am I overthinking it?

content: Great, thank you for the reply. Of course, I realize it’s still a bit early to pin down the exact mechanics or design. But it’s interesting to see that you’ve explored this possibility. I also think it would be highly appealing from a guild perspective - especially for high-tier habs who would benefit from pooling the players resources. I look forward to seeing this topic come up for discussion. Thanks.

author: Lysimach | Make Polis GreatMUD
content: @SW4GN3R seeing the positive reception of the claynosaurs news (giving 15% of their company ownership to nft holders, if I understood correctly), is there a chance that polis holders will own a piece of atmta (could be less than 15%) sometime in the future? I know the goal is the DAO etc, but have you considered how such a move could positively affect the company? For example, if people know they actually own a portion of the company, they could be more inclined to buy the token => demand raises token price => gives atmta more potential revenue from token sales.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: I mean, this is largely what we're already doing, with a larger promise in turning over the SA IP. That is the primary asset on ATMTA's balance sheet. We aren't building any other games or products. I have a different vision in mind for how ATMTA captures value in the future, but the value of ATMTA is driven by the underlying SA IP, which we already intend to transfer to the DAO.

reply.preview: I mean, this is largely what we're already doing, with a larger promise in turning over the SA IP. That is the primary asset on ATMTA's balance sheet. We aren't building any other games or products. I have a different vision in mind for how ATMTA captures value in the future, but the value of ATMTA is driven by the underlying SA IP, which we already intend to transfer to the DAO.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: I think the distinction is that Claynosaurs is offering company equity, while SA's original model is DAO governance over game/ecosystem through POLIS/PVP. It's NOT about POLIS holders owning ATMTA.   That brings the value-capture question: If PVP eventually receives ATLAS from in-game taxes/fees (as planned), it only works if real gameplay demand and ATLAS sinks grow alonside it. Otherwise, it risks becoming constant ATLAS sell pressure instead of sustainable value for POLIS holders.    The believe the old economics paper described the minting (inflation) and burning of ATLAS (DAO Treasury Management) based on user growth and adoption. If this is still valid, eventually the DAO could vote for this model to be enabled - thus possibly affecting supply of ATLAS and its value, is this correct?

reply.preview: I think the distinction is that Claynosaurs is offering company equity, while SA's original model is DAO governance over game/ecosystem through POLIS/PVP. It's NOT about POLIS holders owning ATMTA. That brings the value-capture question: If PVP eventually receives ATLAS from in-game taxes/fees (as planned), it only works if real gameplay demand and ATLAS sinks grow alonside it. Otherwise, it risks becoming constant ATLAS sell pressure instead of sustainable value for POLIS holders. The believe the old economics paper described the minting (inflation) and burning of ATLAS (DAO Treasury Management) based on user growth and adoption. If this is still valid, eventually the DAO could vote for this model to be enabled - thus possibly affecting supply of ATLAS and its value, is this correct?

author: ZiggyMUD
content: This is missing the point though. They are retaining the IP rights, so equity potentially makes sense. We aren’t, so it doesn’t. There is still a proposal for ownership under our model, but it is ownership over an asset (IP), instead of an entity. That aside, I am curious to see how these markets evolve. Offering company equity in the form of tokens 2 years ago would have immediately gotten these projects sued by the SEC. Everything is clearly much looser at the moment, but that could also change with the next US administration.   The implication under our model is not that ATLAS is distributed to POLIS holders (though I do want to see that one day), but rather that ALL game revenues flow to the DAO.

author: Lysimach | Make Polis GreatMUD
content: Agreed. I think we're saying the same thing, but I framed it too much around PVP yield. The core distinction is ownership of an entity vs ownership of the SA game asset/ecosystem.   Claynosaurs retaining the IP makes equity more logical. The SA model is different bc the DAO/Foundation path is meant to put game/IP/revenue governance under POLIS/PVP, not give holders ATMTA equity. That's clear.  My ATLAS point was about the later/future value capture. If all game revenue flows to the DAO, then the DAO can choose whether to reinvest, burn/recirculate ATLAS, fund development, or eventually distribute some value to PVP (if legally & governance approved). That last part is what I find the most interesting.  BUT it's only sustainable if actual gameplay demand and ATLAS sinks grow in parallel.   I hope that explains my view better.

reply.preview: Agreed. I think we're saying the same thing, but I framed it too much around PVP yield. The core distinction is ownership of an entity vs ownership of the SA game asset/ecosystem. Claynosaurs retaining the IP makes equity more logical. The SA model is different bc the DAO/Foundation path is meant to put game/IP/revenue governance under POLIS/PVP, not give holders ATMTA equity. That's clear. My ATLAS point was about the later/future value capture. If all game revenue flows to the DAO, then the DAO can choose whether to reinvest, burn/recirculate ATLAS, fund development, or eventually distribute some value to PVP (if legally & governance approved). That last part is what I find the most interesting. BUT it's only sustainable if actual gameplay demand and ATLAS sinks grow in parallel. I hope that explains my view better.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: You have given strong indication that at some point ATMTA will turn things over to the DAO in the future is that a commitment? If you tokenize the ATMTA and sell it or a portion of it how would that affect Polis.

reply.preview: You have given strong indication that at some point ATMTA will turn things over to the DAO in the future is that a commitment? If you tokenize the ATMTA and sell it or a portion of it how would that affect Polis.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: The architecture is pretty straightforward. We deliver all Star Atlas IP and Star Atlas related revenues to the DAO. In return, we pick up a game development contract and a fixed portion of those future (perpetual) revenues.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: We're trading 100% of value for some lower percentage. That's the value of owning POLIS. You get the 100% minus ATMTA share. And then at ATMTA, depending on how we scale, we can work on other projects and IPs in the future that create more value for ATMTA shareholders, but unrelated to our work on SA.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: So in simple terms:    ATMTA gives up the Star Atlas asset/revenue base.The DAO/Foundation gets stewardship of the asset/IP and most of the economics.,ATMTA keeps value through a development contract and revshare.,POLIS captures value by governing the Star Atlas asset/revenue layer, not by owning ATMTA.,, One important caveat: Revenue flowing to the DAO is not the same as revenue being distributed to POLIS holders. The Foundation documents restrict distributions to tokenholders based on token ownership, so any future direct value transfer tied to PVP would require careful legal structuring (if not a full restructuring of the rules)., Feel free to clarify any point if I'm misunderstanding anything. I want to be able to answer intelligently if I'm asked in public. Thanks you.

reply.preview: So in simple terms: ATMTA gives up the Star Atlas asset/revenue base.The DAO/Foundation gets stewardship of the asset/IP and most of the economics. , ATMTA keeps value through a development contract and revshare. , POLIS captures value by governing the Star Atlas asset/revenue layer, not by owning ATMTA. , , One important caveat: Revenue flowing to the DAO is not the same as revenue being distributed to POLIS holders. The Foundation documents restrict distributions to tokenholders based on token ownership, so any future direct value transfer tied to PVP would require careful legal structuring (if not a full restructuring of the rules). , Feel free to clarify any point if I'm misunderstanding anything. I want to be able to answer intelligently if I'm asked in public. Thanks you.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: In-game "revsharing" is analogous to in-game ATLAS emissions, from my perspective. This is a separate question than top-level DAO revenue distribution from the treasury to POLIS/PVP holders.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Also worth noting, the ATLAS locker will appy benefits to fee-reduction in the future as well. So even if the base fee increases with the fee-on-top model, players will be able to get up to something like an 85% reduction to it, like they do with the marketplace fees.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: Well, the in-game PVP-based democracy module is all based on game activity. 100% of that revenue goes to the DAO today, not ATMTA. There are at least two routes to determining how that fee revenue is carved up, and both might be options within the democracy module: Fee-on-top: People vote to add a fee on top of base fees, within a fee-range guardrail. The fee-on-top funds the pool that is distributed to players with diplomats installed in the democracy module,Fee-carve-out: People vote on a percentage of fee taken out of the base fee. In this model, the total max fee remains the same, but the DAO receives less overall because a portion is carved out and paid to players with diplomats installed,

reply.preview: Well, the in-game PVP-based democracy module is all based on game activity. 100% of that revenue goes to the DAO today, not ATMTA. There are at least two routes to determining how that fee revenue is carved up, and both might be options within the democracy module: Fee-on-top: People vote to add a fee on top of base fees, within a fee-range guardrail. The fee-on-top funds the pool that is distributed to players with diplomats installed in the democracy module , Fee-carve-out: People vote on a percentage of fee taken out of the base fee. In this model, the total max fee remains the same, but the DAO receives less overall because a portion is carved out and paid to players with diplomats installed ,

author: Chet Roberts [ROME]
content: I don't have the details of either Democracy Module Option. But, my first thought is about the guardrails. Without strong guardrails that prevent large voters from setting fee parameters that overpay diplomats at the expense of DAO treasury revenue, ATLAS sinks, or other game activity, this could become an unfair mechanic for the DAO Treasury or smaller players. IMO: It would be great if the community studied the model before it's implemented.   QUESTION:  What guardrails will prevent high-PVP voters from setting an option (fee-on-top or fee-carve-out) parameters that disproportionately reward their own diplomats while reducing DAO treasury revenue or making gameplay more expansive for smaller players?

author: Chet Roberts [ROME]
content: "alongside the gameplay mechanics of controlling a T5 king system" ? This is something only a faction or maybe a guild like Rome can do. My mind is thinking politics on a local, State and Federal level all at once.

author: [AEP] Funcracker [AEP]
content: I’ve shared details on the mechanics previously, but the guardrails are that 1) we will define the available parameters for voting, so users can only vote to select within a reasonable range that we define, and 2) we have asset ownership requirements that include crew, player habs, and ships to move that crew, alongside the gameplay mechanics of controlling a T5 king system. So people that exclusively hold POLIS and PVP can’t even participate

So people that exclusively hold POLIS and PVP can’t even participate

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Happy to see Crew Habs made it in!    During our discussion, I got the feeling you are approaching this from the point of "The diplomats need to live somewhere", which def can be argued for.  I, however, would like to make the case for the "ownership of stake" in a region, before you are able to meddle with the governance. In other words, if you live there, have a home business, or regular business in the region, and own any kind of real-estate, then you get to vote. In short: both Crew Habs and Claim Stakes (+Cultivation Claims) would allow you to send diplomats (aka lobbyists) to the regional governance center to argue your case.  It's a bit like: If you don't live here in a registered, official fashion, or have a business located here, you do not get to vote on local affairs.  From that perspective, it's not about housing the diplomats, it's about matching your registered "stake" in a region with your max voting power.

reply.preview: Happy to see Crew Habs made it in! During our discussion, I got the feeling you are approaching this from the point of "The diplomats need to live somewhere", which def can be argued for. I, however, would like to make the case for the "ownership of stake" in a region, before you are able to meddle with the governance. In other words, if you live there, have a home business, or regular business in the region, and own any kind of real-estate, then you get to vote. In short: both Crew Habs and Claim Stakes (+Cultivation Claims) would allow you to send diplomats (aka lobbyists) to the regional governance center to argue your case. It's a bit like: If you don't live here in a registered, official fashion, or have a business located here, you do not get to vote on local affairs. From that perspective, it's not about housing the diplomats, it's about matching your registered "stake" in a region with your max voting power.

author: Xap✨ [STAR]
content: Yes, I think that was a good suggestion. We're going to be leveraging CSS Habs for these requirements so we can provide a little utility to those who purchased them way back in 2022. Also potentially helps us sell more of them going forward, until we can get the true utility for them on CSS's in UE. While there is a lot of ways we can expand on these democracy module mechanics in the future, I'm pretty comfortable with where we landed on Crew + Habs + Transportation requirements as an MVP, that still let's us unlock basic regional governance and revenue distribution. I would absolutely love to see alternative options for players in utilizing the local treasury for things beyond ATLAS distributions. I'm specifically thinking about security contracts using the rental system to hire fleets to patrol a starbase on behalf of that system. But there are endless options we could introduce.  Not that you asked, but a note on these CSS Claims/Habs. Currently, they are (unintentionally) misrepresentative on the marketplace. We sold these as "CSS Plot Claims" during Start Sequence in 2022, implying that you were purchasing the land on the faction CSS. They came with all kinds of benefits in terms of priority land selection, a "free" hab, and bunch of smaller NFTs to enhance your real estate. However, on the marketplace, all of the imagery and the categorization imply you purchased "Housing." What I have suggested we do internally is not retroactively recategorize everything, but instead treat all of those purchases as Housing units that can be placed at any starbase (or even planet surface) in the galaxy. All of the original perks that were promoted during Start Sequence will be maintained. However, instead of receiving a free house/hab with your CSS claim purchase, you'll get a free CSS Claim equivalent to the size of your hab tier, and still get priority treatment on selecting a plot when that feature is released. We still have the snapshot data from back then for those eligible. So all of the units in player inventory or on the marketplace today are converting to generalized housing to be used anywhere in game.

reply.preview: Yes, I think that was a good suggestion. We're going to be leveraging CSS Habs for these requirements so we can provide a little utility to those who purchased them way back in 2022. Also potentially helps us sell more of them going forward, until we can get the true utility for them on CSS's in UE. While there is a lot of ways we can expand on these democracy module mechanics in the future, I'm pretty comfortable with where we landed on Crew + Habs + Transportation requirements as an MVP, that still let's us unlock basic regional governance and revenue distribution. I would absolutely love to see alternative options for players in utilizing the local treasury for things beyond ATLAS distributions. I'm specifically thinking about security contracts using the rental system to hire fleets to patrol a starbase on behalf of that system. But there are endless options we could introduce. Not that you asked, but a note on these CSS Claims/Habs. Currently, they are (unintentionally) misrepresentative on the marketplace. We sold these as "CSS Plot Claims" during Start Sequence in 2022, implying that you were purchasing the land on the faction CSS. They came with all kinds of benefits in terms of priority land selection, a "free" hab, and bunch of smaller NFTs to enhance your real estate. However, on the marketplace, all of the imagery and the categorization imply you purchased "Housing." What I have suggested we do internally is not retroactively recategorize everything, but instead treat all of those purchases as Housing units that can be placed at any starbase (or even planet surface) in the galaxy. All of the original perks that were promoted during Start Sequence will be maintained. However, instead of receiving a free house/hab with your CSS claim purchase, you'll get a free CSS Claim equivalent to the size of your hab tier, and still get priority treatment on selecting a plot when that feature is released. We still have the snapshot data from back then for those eligible. So all of the units in player inventory or on the marketplace today are converting to generalized housing to be used anywhere in game.

author: Xap✨ [STAR]
content: The habs/houses are not going to be faction specific. Any hab can be placed anywhere on the map. The CSS Plot Claims that were promoted during Start Sequence will be faction specific. When that feature is released, people who purchased in the promotion window will have first rights to claim a free (tier equivalent) CSS Plot on the respective faction CSS.  Also, hab placement is not permanent. You should/will be able to recover a hab and place it somewhere else if you desire to move it.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: I’ve shared details on the mechanics previously, but the guardrails are that 1) we will define the available parameters for voting, so users can only vote to select within a reasonable range that we define, and 2) we have asset ownership requirements that include crew, player habs, and ships to move that crew, alongside the gameplay mechanics of controlling a T5 king system. So people that exclusively hold POLIS and PVP can’t even participate

So people that exclusively hold POLIS and PVP can’t even participate

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: If requirements are low enough for small players, they are likely trivial for whales. The POLIS/PVP-only-whales are in the minority.   So the questions become:  1) After a player meets the Crew + Habs + Transportation + other minor asset + some Active Play requirements, how exactly is voting power linked to reward payouts calculated? 2) And will there be caps, diminishing returns, or anti-sybil rules so whales/guilds cannot simply stack Habs, Crew, ships, and PVP to capture most of the local fee revenue?,

author: *Solaris* 4NUNN4KI
content:
Hello,

I have a question about the skill tree and specializations based on fields of activity.
I seem to recall hearing during a Brew session that the general idea was to - more or less - require/encourage players to focus on one or more (but not all) fields of activity, and that they would have to make choices because they wouldn’t be able to unlock all skills.

I’d like to know if this is indeed the plan (and if I’ve understood it correctly), and if so, how it would work in practice.
For example, if a player decides to max out their skills in combat and datarunning, would they be unable to do the same in mining or crafting?
I’m asking because I’m thinking about some suggestions, but I’d like to be sure I’ve understood the general idea first.
Thank you.

author: *Solaris* 4NUNN4KI
content: I will jump in and let Michael/danny share any extra thoughts, but the idea is mostly aligned with what you said. You can always increase your performance in any specialization in Star atlas, so you will never truly max out any career node. Just as an example, we could have a node that gives a permanent boost to your scanning skill that you can allocate an unlimited amount of points, so even if you would reach level 1k and spend all in scanning, you can always keep improving those infinite nodes and become better in that front

reply.preview: I will jump in and let Michael/danny share any extra thoughts, but the idea is mostly aligned with what you said. You can always increase your performance in any specialization in Star atlas, so you will never truly max out any career node. Just as an example, we could have a node that gives a permanent boost to your scanning skill that you can allocate an unlimited amount of points, so even if you would reach level 1k and spend all in scanning, you can always keep improving those infinite nodes and become better in that front

author: *Solaris* 4NUNN4KI
content:
Alternatively, we could envision the possibility of maxing out all the careers (the default ones found in the global skill tree) and applying permanent boosts - as you mentioned - based on various factors (such as crafting specific items, unlocking reputation with aligned NPCs, etc.).

The idea would be to allow every player to fully max out all the default skill trees so that no one is limited/not incentivized from purchasing specific assets or exploring particular activities at any stage of their Player-Account's life, while still allowing for perma (or temporary) boosts to certain types of careers/activities at specific times that the player could choose based on their current preferences.

If, for a period of time, they prefer to mine or manage their buildings, they can focus on those specializations (wiht boosts for exemple).
And if, during another period, they prefer to engage in combat, they can focus on that activity.
But this should not impose a constraint related to a discretionary choice that the player must make regarding the default skill trees.

That's pretty much what I mean.
Basically, what's set in stone - namely, the ability to max out all existing skills in the general tree - remains open to everyone (there are no limitations on the progression of all default base skills).
And temporary or permanent boosts are where the player's discretion should come into play.

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: Okay, yes that’s pretty much what I understood, though I hadn't grasped the nuance regarding potential boosts. While waiting for details from Michael or Danny, let me share what this has led me to consider.  In a single-player game, it makes sense to consider maxing out the player's account by forcing them to steer their unique character's specialization toward a specific class. However, in an MMO like Star Atlas which involves a wide range of activities and numerous crew members to manage, I believe it would be a mistake to max out careers at the Player-Account level. It would make more sense, in my view, to max out specific activity areas at the Crew-Member level rather than the general Player-Account level.  There are several reasons for this line of reasoning. First, if the player account were restricted from specializing in absolutely every activity, there would be no incentive to purchase ships (or many other assets) for specializations the Player-Account doesn't support. For instance, if a player maxes out their specialization in mining and crafting but cannot earn enough reputation or XP to advance in other areas (like combat or data running), they would have no reason to buy combat ships or data runners. Yet, the goal isn't to limit the potential purchases a player might make. It would be akin to the studio shooting itself in the foot by voluntarily restricting asset sales opportunities for a vast number of players simply because they cannot specialize in every field.  Secondly, tying specializations at the Crews level rather than the individual Player-Account would be 1/ much more realistic (meaning you could have Crews specialized in combat, others in crafting, others in data running, etc.), and 2/ it would add even more gameplay depth. Logically, specializations should operate at the Crew level rather than the Player-Account level.  I don't think players should be prevented from specializing in every field of activity simultaneously - precisely because it is the Crew members who specialize, not the player himself. This approach would open up the entire asset market to all players, avoiding a situation where certain assets are "restricted" to specific players simply because they were forced to specialize.  That is why I see more the players able to specialize in absolutely every activity the game offers - perhaps through secondary skill trees (at the Crew level), or by not limiting the player's global skill tree, or via some other method (to be determined).  The goal would be to avoid stifling the player experience, allowing everyone to explore and fully appreciate the game's depth, while also ensuring that players aren't restricted in the assets they can purchase due to their specialization choices. I’m not sure if I’m making myself clear (sorry) and what your thoughts on this might be.

author: *Solaris* 4NUNN4KI
content: I think creating deep opportunities for specialization is one of the most important game design decisions.  I sent @bunthius these 2 ideas several months ago.  Both of these could allow players to specialize is one type of resource and gain edges over time.  IDEA #1: Dedication XP for Resource Extraction Specialization  One of the most important things we need in C4 is trickle down economics.  We need whales to buy stuff from smaller players.    One way is A SECOND type of council rank power that is NOT based on asset sizes.  It would be a second type of  perk/rank system  that is TIME BASED.  Could be a "Dedication XP".  Daily gameplay gives 1 DXP.  30 DXP = 1 specialization boost. Make it very powerful.  DXP points could be spent on boosting extraction rate for 1 type of resource.  Make the specialization depth infinite (like the perk system).  IDEA #2:  Mining Specialization   Create the ability to create specialized mining rigs that buff extraction of a certain resource.  E.G. A diamond mining rig (T1-T5).  This is where with 100 resources, it would open up a tremendous amount of niche opportunity for a massive amount of players.

author: ⪛⦿⫺ZeSKK [STAR]
content: The council rank specialization is one thing, crew career paths are another

author: ⪛⦿⫺ZeSKK [STAR]
content: Yes, I agree with that. Specialization remains a crucial aspect of the game and its economy. I simply want to avoid restricting the baseline skills of the default general tree too early; I wouldn't want to limit the experience prematurely or curtail the gameplay possibilities available to players as they discover the game over time. That said, I fully agree that specialization must - as a subsequent step - be meaningful and create a genuine distinction from the base skill tree. The ideas you’ve raised are very interesting. Thanks for sharing them.

reply.preview: Yes, I agree with that. Specialization remains a crucial aspect of the game and its economy. I simply want to avoid restricting the baseline skills of the default general tree too early; I wouldn't want to limit the experience prematurely or curtail the gameplay possibilities available to players as they discover the game over time. That said, I fully agree that specialization must - as a subsequent step - be meaningful and create a genuine distinction from the base skill tree. The ideas you’ve raised are very interesting. Thanks for sharing them.

author: ⪛⦿⫺ZeSKK [STAR]
content: Gated/supported

author: *Solaris* 4NUNN4KI
content: The way I see this is something like this:  you council nodes is your corporation knowledge and influence in the world of Star atlas.  It represents the limits of what you know and are able to access.  Crewmembers can be leveled up and reach what your council rank allows you. So for example if you are a master of combat this will allow your crewmembers to reach the strongest skills and abilities that their individual combat paths could reach.  Your crewmembers would be gated by what you have achieved in our council ranks

content: I'm so tired of having to go through Phantom because I was an early adopter of Ledger. Are we ever going to fix us being able to pick our appropriate derivation paths? content: The Star Atlas anime series is the right direction, @Gary Sanchez (AD Veh).
